result: discarded — LLM vision too noisy for n=200
discarded — r=+0.30 vs keep but regression noise

notes: No frame diff analysis implemented. Could extract from video files.

result: discarded — 80% have faces, no predictive power
discarded — collinear with retention, negative delta

notes: Not yet scored. Could use vision API.
result: has_text_overlay kept — no text overlay in first frame = more views (CV +0.029). But reversed: clean thumbnails predict success.
CV +0.057 alongside retention. Fixes duration confound.
STRONGEST SIGNAL. CV +0.093. Retention curve biggest drop = virality signature.
Non-linear keep effect. CV +0.031. Returns accelerate above 80%.
Non-linear midpoint retention. CV +0.019.
Subscriber vs non-sub retention gap. CV +0.020. Broad appeal signal.
Words/sec in hook. CV +0.014. Visual hooks > talky hooks.
Title pattern. CV +0.036, 10/10 seeds. Channel viral formula #1.
LLM category. CV +0.021, 10/10 seeds. Channel viral formula #2.

hypothesis: Audio energy/loudness in first 3 seconds correlates with keep rate
method: Extract audio from video files, compute RMS energy in first 3s
notes: No audio analysis tools available without ffmpeg/librosa dependencies

hypothesis: Videos with a clear tension-resolution arc in transcript get more views
method: Use LLM to score tension arc quality from full transcript
notes: May need larger dataset
result: Hook tension/promise/speed LLM scores tested. None improve v16 model. LLM content scores too noisy at n=203.

hypothesis: Thumbnails with text overlay get higher keep rate
method: Vision API to detect text in first frame
notes: Similar to text_overlay_yn
result: has_text_overlay from frame analysis kept as v16 feature. Vision API text detection too noisy.

hypothesis: High-keep indestructible/superhero videos have disproportionate virality
method: Test keep × concept_flag interactions
notes: Concept flags may need larger sample
result: stakes_x_duration (concept × duration interaction) kept. indestructible_x_prev_keep already in v13.

hypothesis: Do indestructible/superhero patterns work across channels?
method: Test on data from other creator channels
notes: Requires external data

hypothesis: Day of week affects initial algorithm push and total views
method: Extract from metadata uploadDate, test as categorical
notes: Free feature from metadata
result: Upload day tested — Saturday avg 14M but CV Δ < 0.01. Discarded.

hypothesis: Day3-7 average views / Day1 views captures algorithmic acceleration. Higher ratio = algorithm amplifying after initial test.
method: Compute avg(dailyViews[2:7])/dailyViews[0] from analytics
notes: r=+0.37, r_partial=+0.34. Outlier analysis: 30.4x for top10 vs 0.7x for bottom10. Independent of keep.
result: KEPT in v18 → v20. CV +0.048. Log transform (view_accel_log) is strongest single predictor in v20.

hypothesis: Ratio of late (75-100%) to early (0-25%) retention area. Videos that hold viewers in the back half disproportionately outperform.
method: Compute avg(curve[75%:])/avg(curve[:25%]) from retentionCurve
notes: r=+0.39 (203), r_partial=+0.29. On full 370 videos: r_partial=+0.55. Massive signal on larger sample.
result: Discarded — r=+0.31 but CV Δ=-0.016 on v20. Collinear with existing curve features.

hypothesis: Lower retention@3s / peak ratio = more views. Big initial spike (replay behavior) matters more than smooth hook retention.
method: Compute curve[3] / max(curve[0:5]) from retentionCurve
notes: r=-0.40, r_partial=-0.27 (203). On 370: r_partial=-0.49. Counter-intuitive: hook drops PREDICT virality.
result: Discarded — r=-0.25, CV Δ=-0.012 on v20.

hypothesis: Higher Shannon entropy of retention curve distribution = more views. Information-rich curves (varied retention) outperform monotonic decay.
method: Compute -sum(p*log2(p)) where p=curve[i]/sum(curve) from retentionCurve
notes: r=+0.38, r_partial=+0.29 (203). On 370: r_partial=+0.48. Strong across both sample sizes.
result: Discarded — numerical instability (CV=-215). Scale/collinearity issues.

hypothesis: Higher kurtosis (peakedness) of retention curve = more views. Leptokurtic curves with sharp peaks indicate dramatic content.
method: Compute excess kurtosis of retention curve values
notes: r=+0.35, r_partial=+0.27 (203). On 370: r_partial=+0.48. OUTLIER: top10 kurtosis=5.17 vs bottom10=2.10 (2.47x ratio).
result: Discarded — r=+0.35 but CV Δ=-0.01 on v20. Collinear with max_cliff.

hypothesis: Viral videos do NOT follow exponential view decay. Lower R² of log-linear fit on first 30 days = more views.
method: Fit log(dailyViews) ~ day for first 30 days, compute R² of fit
notes: r=-0.37, r_partial=-0.34. Viral videos have surges, second waves, non-predictable trajectories.
result: Discarded — r=-0.36, CV Δ=-0.001 on v18. Captured by view_accel_log.

hypothesis: derivStd × bodyHold × (1-decayR2) = multi-dimensional narrative quality. Combines dramatic pacing + mid-video retention + non-predictable trajectory.
method: Compute derivStd * bodyHold * (1 - trajectoryDecayR2)
notes: r=+0.46, r_partial=+0.43. BEST NEW COMBO SIGNAL. Three independent dimensions in one feature.
result: Discarded — r=+0.46, CV Δ=-0.002 on v18. Collinear with view_accel_log + curve features.

hypothesis: Previous video views × max retention cliff interaction. Channel momentum amplifies dramatic content.
method: Compute log10(prev_video_views) * max_cliff
notes: r=+0.46, r_partial=+0.40. Momentum × drama interaction outperforms either alone.
result: Marginal — CV Δ=+0.006. Below threshold.

hypothesis: Late/early retention ratio × derivative std. Videos that hold late AND have dramatic pacing get disproportionate views.
method: Compute lateEarlyRatio * derivStd
notes: r=+0.42, r_partial=+0.38. Two independent retention quality dimensions.
result: Discarded — CV Δ=-0.002 on v18.

hypothesis: Ratio of post-peak trough to peak daily views. Higher = more sustained viewership after initial spike.
method: Compute min(dailyViews[peakDay+1:])/max(dailyViews[0:14]) from dailyViews
notes: r=+0.23, r_partial=+0.20. Sustained viewership after peak predicts total.
result: Discarded — CV Δ<0.005 on v20.

hypothesis: Watch minutes per view on Day 1 = engagement quality before algorithm kicks in. Higher quality early viewers predict total views.
method: Compute dailyViews[0].watchMinutes / dailyViews[0].views
notes: r=+0.31, r_partial=+0.22. Day 1 watch quality predicts long-term performance.
result: wm_per_view_trend KEPT in v19 pair with view_accel_log, but dropped in v20.

hypothesis: Fewer retention curve inflection points = smoother narrative = more views.
method: Count sign changes in second derivative of retentionCurve
notes: r=-0.18, r_partial=-0.12 (203). On 370: r_partial=-0.23. Smooth curves outperform jagged ones.
result: Discarded — CV Δ=-0.005 on v20.

hypothesis: Day 1 views as fraction of first 30 days. Lower = algorithm sustained push. Above 15% = death zone (0/27 hit 10M+).
method: dailyViews[0].views / sum(dailyViews[0:30].views)
notes: r=-0.52, r_partial=-0.50. Top trajectory signal after controlling for keep. Spike_decay (>15% day1) = 0% hit rate.
result: KEPT in v18, dropped in v20 backward elimination. Captured by view_accel_log.

hypothesis: Amount retention exceeds 1.0 in first 5 data points = replay behavior intensity. Higher replays in hook = more views.
method: max(retentionCurve[0:5].retention) - 1.0, clamped at 0
notes: r=+0.32, r_partial=+0.19. Replays signal strong hook. Peak replay 1.4+ for top videos.
result: Discarded — CV Δ=-0.008 on v20.

hypothesis: How far through the video before retention drops below 70%. Later drop point = better narrative holding.
method: Find first retentionCurve point < 0.70, return fraction through video
notes: r=+0.30, r_partial=+0.25. Threshold-based signal captures narrative quality differently than average.
result: Discarded — CV Δ=-0.006 on v20.

hypothesis: Lower standard deviation in middle 50% of retention curve = smoother narrative = more views.
method: StdDev of retentionCurve[25%:75%]
notes: r=-0.26, r_partial=-0.19. Smooth middle sections indicate confident pacing.
result: Discarded — CV Δ=-0.002 on v20.

hypothesis: z(kurtosis) + z(viewAccel) - z(day1share) = best 3-signal combo from new discoveries.
method: Z-score normalize each signal, combine with sign correction
notes: r=0.632. Best 3-signal model from ALL new discoveries. Captures curve shape + acceleration + trajectory.

hypothesis: Concave cumulative view curve indicates growing interest/algorithmic push. More concave = more views.
method: Compute area under cumulative view curve / triangle area. <1 = concave growth
notes: r=-0.21, r_partial=-0.18. Captures whether views accelerate or decelerate.

hypothesis: 4-7 day gap between uploads maximizes next video performance. Too fast (1-3d) = 11% hit rate vs 33% at 4-7d.
method: Compute days between consecutive uploads from metadata.uploadDate
notes: Hit rates: 1-3d=11%, 4-7d=33%, 8-14d=20%, 15-30d=21%. Sweet spot is clear.

hypothesis: FEWER extreme derivative points (>2x avg) in retention curve = more views. Even pacing beats wild swings.
method: Count points where |deriv| > 2 * mean(|deriv|) in retentionCurve
notes: r_partial=-0.27 on 370 videos. Counter-intuitive: viral pacing is controlled drama, not chaos.
result: Discarded — CV Δ=+0.003 (marginal) on v20.

hypothesis: Top videos separate from bottom videos primarily in the TAIL of the retention curve (last 20%). Tail retention is the true differentiator.
method: Compare normalized retention curves at 20 points across view tiers
notes: Top-bottom gap at 5%=0.015, at 50%=0.074, at 95%=0.097. Divergence increases through video. Implications: optimize endings, not just hooks.

hypothesis: Videos that are part of a series (part 2, sequel, continuation) perform differently
method: Detect series indicators in title (part, episode, #, continued) and test as binary + interaction with prev_views
notes: Could capture audience expectation effects

hypothesis: Videos featuring collaborators/guests have different view patterns
method: Detect mentions of other people in title or AI summary. Test as binary flag.
notes: Collaborations expand audience reach

hypothesis: Shannon entropy of binned retention derivatives captures pacing complexity
method: Compute derivative of retention curve, bin into 10 buckets, compute entropy
notes: Different from deriv_std — captures distribution shape not just spread

hypothesis: Videos with concepts dissimilar from recent uploads get algorithm boost
method: Use LLM embedding similarity between consecutive video titles/ideas. Lower similarity = fresher concept
notes: Requires embedding API. May capture algorithm fatigue effect.

hypothesis: Previous video's engagement quality predicts next video's views better than raw view count
method: Compute prev_engagement_rate, prev_comment_sentiment, prev_share_ratio as features
notes: Hot audience (high engagement) may be more receptive to next video

hypothesis: Time gap between uploads affects views (optimal upload frequency exists)
method: Compute days_since_last_upload from metadata uploadDate. Test as linear and quadratic.
notes: Free feature from metadata. May capture audience anticipation effect.

hypothesis: Different content types have different optimal retention curve shapes
method: Cluster curves using DTW distance, assign cluster labels, test as categorical
notes: Previous attempt used basic k-means on raw values. DTW may capture shape better.

hypothesis: Model improves significantly with more data (n>500)
method: Check if additional videos exist in video_data/ beyond the 203 in signals-dataset.json
notes: 2356 video_data dirs vs 203 in dataset. Expanding could dramatically improve model stability.

hypothesis: Second derivative std of retention curve captures 'pacing acceleration jerkiness'. Videos with variable pacing RATE-OF-CHANGE (not just variable pacing) get more views.
method: Compute second derivative of retentionCurve, take std dev
notes: r=+0.21, r_partial=+0.28 (203). On 370: r=+0.22. Different from deriv_std (1st derivative) — captures acceleration of pacing changes. Top10 avg=0.016 vs bottom10=0.010 (1.67x).

hypothesis: Longest streak of consecutive daily view increases in first 30 days. Longer sustained growth = algorithm actively pushing harder over time.
method: Count max consecutive days where views[i] > views[i-1] in first 30 dailyViews entries
notes: r=+0.23, r_partial=+0.25 (201). On 370: r=+0.23. Captures sustained algorithmic acceleration distinct from single-ratio metrics. Top10 avg 3.0 vs full avg 3.8.

hypothesis: Max-min retention across entire curve. Bigger range = bigger initial spike AND steeper drop = replay behavior + dramatic content combined.
method: Compute max(retentionCurve) - min(retentionCurve)
notes: r=+0.11, r_partial=+0.24 (202). On 370: r=+0.12. Captures combined hook spike amplitude + end drop. Different from max_cliff (single biggest drop).

hypothesis: Harmonic mean of retention curve penalizes low points more heavily than arithmetic mean. Videos without any severe dips outperform.
method: Compute n / sum(1/curve[i]) for positive retention values
notes: r=+0.29, r_partial=+0.21 (202). On 370: r=+0.47. MASSIVE on full set. Captures 'no weak spots' quality differently than avg retention.

hypothesis: Fraction of video where retention stays above 80%. 'High-quality viewing fraction' — how much of the video truly holds the audience.
method: Count retentionCurve points > 0.80, divide by total points
notes: r=+0.27, r_partial=+0.21 (202). On 370: r=+0.47. MASSIVE on full set. Tier: 100M+=0.69, 10-50M=0.62, 1-5M=0.53, sub1M=0.44. Clear tier separation.

hypothesis: IQR/median of retention curve = relative quartile spread. Higher dispersion means wider range of viewer experience relative to typical viewer.
method: Compute (ret_75pct - ret_25pct) / ret_50pct from retentionCurve
notes: r=+0.34, r_partial=+0.25 (202). On 370: r=+0.47. Captures normalized curve spread. Viral videos have WIDER relative spread of viewer retention.

hypothesis: First 3 days as fraction of 30-day views. STRONGER variant of day1_share. Lower = algorithm sustained push over longer period.
method: sum(dailyViews[0:3].views) / sum(dailyViews[0:30].views)
notes: r=-0.41, r_partial=-0.37 (201). Tier: 100M+=0.31, 10-50M=0.12, 1-5M=0.24, sub1M=0.36. Strengthens day1_share concept with wider window.

hypothesis: Day 3 views / Day 1 views captures very early algorithmic acceleration. 3-day window captures the initial 'test audience' → 'broad push' transition.
method: dailyViews[2].views / dailyViews[0].views
notes: r=+0.31, r_partial=+0.29 (201). OUTLIER KING: Top10 avg=13.78x vs bottom10=1.16x (11.86x difference). Single biggest outlier ratio found in any signal.

hypothesis: Lower daily view coefficient of variation = more consistent viewing pattern = more total views. Algorithm rewards steady performers.
method: std(dailyViews[0:30].views) / mean(dailyViews[0:30].views)
notes: r=-0.16, r_partial=-0.16 (201). Modest but independent of keep. Top10 avg=1.35 vs bottom10=1.89. Consistent daily views pattern.

hypothesis: Fewer comments per 10k views = MORE total views. Viral videos reach passive viewers who watch but don't comment.
method: (comments / totalViews) * 10000
notes: r=-0.20, r_partial=-0.14 (203). On 370: r=-0.20. Tier: 100M+=0.34, sub1M=3.92 (11.5x). Same dilution pattern as like_ratio_inverse.

hypothesis: Lower subscriber loss as fraction of subscriber gain = better content quality. Polarizing content loses subs relative to gains.
method: subscribersLost / subscribersGained
notes: r=-0.17, r_partial=-0.13 (203). On 370: r=-0.22. Modest but captures content quality / audience satisfaction dimension.

hypothesis: Shorter hook as fraction of total video = more views. Efficient hooks that get to the point faster outperform.
method: (hookSeg.endTime - hookSeg.startTime) / metadata.duration from aiAnalysis segments
notes: r=-0.10, r_partial=-0.12 (157). 100M+ hookDuration=5s vs others=7s. Shorter hooks get to the setup faster. Hook should be ~12% of video, not 15%+.

hypothesis: Day 2 / Day 1 view ratio captures earliest algorithmic signal. Higher = algorithm testing to broader audience immediately.
method: dailyViews[1].views / dailyViews[0].views
notes: r=+0.14, r_partial=+0.13 (201). Top10 avg=7.5x vs bottom10=2.2x (3.4x ratio). Weaker than day3_growth but captures earliest algorithm decision.

hypothesis: Higher coefficient of variation in AI-detected narrative segments = more dramatic pacing = more views. Uneven segment lengths signal narrative tension.
method: StdDev(segment durations) / Mean(segment durations) from aiAnalysis segments
notes: Tier: 100M+=0.47 vs sub1M=0.30 (1.57x). Top videos have more varied narrative rhythm. n=192 (not all videos have segments).

hypothesis: Videos with a question in the first 10 seconds get more views. Questions create open loops that demand resolution.
method: Detect question marks or question words (how/what/why/can/could/would/will/do) in first 10s of transcript
notes: 100M+ = 100% have questions in first 10s. On 370: r=+0.19. Questions in hook are table stakes for top tier.

hypothesis: Shorter titles get more views. Concise, punchy titles outperform descriptive ones. Algorithm may favor shorter titles in thumbnails.
method: Word count of metadata.title
notes: 100M+ avg 4 words vs others 6-7. Weak correlation (r=+0.06 overall) but STRONG tier signal. May interact with concept type.

hypothesis: Retention at 90% mark (near the end) captures 'did viewers stay for the payoff?' signal. Critical completion point.
method: retentionCurve[floor(n*0.90)].retention
notes: r=+0.32, r_partial=+0.23 (203). On 370: r=+0.505. MASSIVE on full set — near-end retention is a top-tier signal. Captures payoff delivery.

hypothesis: Retention at 95% mark — the very last moments. Videos where viewers stay through the conclusion have paid off their promise.
method: retentionCurve[floor(n*0.95)].retention
notes: r=+0.32, r_partial=+0.24 (203). On 370: r=+0.501. Near-identical to ret_90pct. The last 5-10% of the video is the virality differentiator.

hypothesis: Higher fraction of views from non-subscribers = more total views. This is OUTCOME (algorithm pushes viral videos to non-subs) but captures 'algorithm reach factor'.
method: nonSubscriberViews / totalViews
notes: r=+0.85, r_partial=+0.82 (202). STRONGEST correlation found in any signal. But OUTCOME/CIRCULAR. Top10=97.8% nonSub vs bottom10=74.9%. Measures algorithm reach directly.

hypothesis: Max consecutive growth days × keep rate interaction. Sustained algorithm push on high-keep content produces amplified virality.
method: maxConsecGrowthDays * keep
notes: r=+0.33, r_partial=+0.24 (203). Best combo signal from new discoveries. Algorithm growth + content quality interaction.

hypothesis: Lower coefficient of variation in watch-minutes-per-view across first 30 days = more views. Consistent watch quality = algorithm reward signal.
method: std(dailyViews[i].watchMinutes/dailyViews[i].views) / mean(...) for first 30 days
notes: r=-0.37, r_partial=-0.40 (366). STRONG and independent of keep. Watch quality consistency is a distinct dimension from total views or retention. Algorithm rewards predictable engagement quality.

hypothesis: Ratio of retention at 95% mark to retention at 10% mark. Higher = better payoff delivery. Videos that hold viewers near the end relative to early (post-spike) levels outperform.
method: retentionCurve[floor(n*0.95)] / retentionCurve[floor(n*0.10)]
notes: r=+0.54 (370), r_partial=+0.31 (203). MASSIVE raw correlation on full set. Measures 'did the video deliver on its promise?' Captures payoff quality.

hypothesis: Videos with peak views on day 3+ (delayed peak) have 31% 10M+ hit rate vs 0% for early peak (day1, >15% share). Trajectory shape is destiny.
method: Classify trajectory: if peak_day >= 3 in first 14 days → delayed_peak. If peak_day==0 AND d1_share>15% → early_peak.
notes: delayed_peak: n=153, 31% hit 10M+, avg 3.3M. early_peak: n=15, 0% hit rate, avg 0.4M. second_wave: n=100, 14% hit. steady_growth: n=53, 11% hit. Early peaking is a DEATH SENTENCE.

hypothesis: day3Growth + first3Share + deriv2Std are mutually independent (r<0.15 between each pair) and each correlates r>0.28 with views after controlling for keep+retention. Best independent 3-signal set from new discoveries.
method: Z-score normalize each, combine: z(day3Growth) - z(first3Share) + z(deriv2Std)
notes: Cross-correlations: day3Growth×first3Share=-0.11, day3Growth×deriv2Std=0.04, first3Share×deriv2Std=-0.10. Three truly orthogonal signals capturing: early acceleration, sustained trajectory, pacing dynamics.

hypothesis: d2Std × (1-first3Share) × log10(day3Growth) = BEST composite ever found. Three orthogonal dimensions: pacing acceleration jerkiness × sustained trajectory × early growth rate.
expected_signal: r > 0.50 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: Compute deriv2Std * (1 - first3DayShare) * log10(max(1, day3Growth)) for each video
notes: r=+0.22 raw, r_partial=+0.52 (367). BEST NEW SIGNAL. All three components are mutually independent (r<0.15). Captures pre-upload curve quality AND post-upload trajectory. Beats narrative_momentum (r_p=+0.43).

hypothesis: endStartRetentionRatio × log10(day3Growth) = payoff delivery quality × algorithmic acceleration. Did the video pay off its hook AND get pushed?
expected_signal: r > 0.45 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: Compute (ret@95%/ret@10%) * log10(max(1, day3/day1 views))
notes: r=+0.32 raw, r_partial=+0.50 (369). Combines retention payoff with early acceleration. Two strong independent signals multiplied.

hypothesis: endStartRetentionRatio × (1-first3DayShare) = retention quality × sustained trajectory. Videos that deliver on promise AND maintain views over time.
expected_signal: r > 0.40 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: Compute (ret@95%/ret@10%) * (1 - sum(first3DayViews)/sum(first30DayViews))
notes: r=+0.52 raw, r_partial=+0.42 (369). Combines curve quality and trajectory. Strong on both raw and partial.

hypothesis: deriv2Std / wmCV = pacing dynamics efficiency. Higher pacing variation combined with consistent watch quality per view.
expected_signal: r > 0.30 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: Compute deriv2Std / (std(wmPerView)/mean(wmPerView))
notes: r=+0.26 raw, r_partial=+0.36 (367). Novel dimension: pacing quality normalized by watch quality consistency.

hypothesis: Mean retention curve area × (1-first3Share). Overall curve quality × sustained trajectory.
expected_signal: r > 0.35 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: mean(retentionCurve) * (1 - first3DayShare)
notes: r=+0.36 raw, r_partial=+0.38 (369). Simple but effective: overall retention quality × trajectory sustainability.

hypothesis: Higher share-to-comment ratio = content that spreads rather than just gets discussed. Shareability over discussion-worthiness.
expected_signal: r > 0.15 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: shares / comments from analytics
notes: r=+0.03 raw, r_partial=+0.20 (368). Moderate but independent of keep. Captures 'shareable vs debatable' content quality.

hypothesis: Total curvature energy (mean of second derivative squared) of retention curve. Higher = more dynamic pacing transitions = more views.
expected_signal: r > 0.20 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: mean(deriv2[i]^2) for retentionCurve second derivative
notes: r=-0.08 raw, r_partial=+0.24 (370). Sign flips after controlling for keep! Dynamic pacing after accounting for overall quality.

hypothesis: Average curvature magnitude in last 20% of retention curve. Higher end-of-video curvature = stronger conclusion dynamics = more views.
expected_signal: r > 0.15 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: mean(|deriv2[80%:]|) for retentionCurve
notes: r=+0.01 raw, r_partial=+0.21 (370). Only visible after controlling for keep. Conclusion pacing matters independently.

hypothesis: Videos that deviate from the creator's recent average duration get more views. Format novelty in length signals fresh content to the algorithm.
expected_signal: r > 0.15 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: abs(current_duration - mean(prev_3_durations)) / mean(prev_3_durations)
notes: r=+0.08, r_partial=+0.17 (369). Counter-intuitive: format consistency HURTS. Algorithm rewards experimentation in video length.

hypothesis: View trend over last 3 videos (ascending or descending momentum). Building momentum predicts the next video's performance beyond just previous views.
expected_signal: r > 0.15 vs log(views) controlling for keep
method: log10(prev1_views) - log10(prev3_views)
notes: r=+0.07, r_partial=+0.16 (369). Captures momentum direction beyond just level. Sequence of improvements predicts next hit.

hypothesis: Day 3 growth ratio ALONE captures a massive independent dimension. After controlling for keep+retention+maxCliff+keepSq it still holds at r_p=+0.48.
expected_signal: r > 0.45 vs log(views) controlling for model top features
method: log10(max(1, dailyViews[2].views / dailyViews[0].views))
notes: Strongest single new standalone signal. But partially OUTCOME — fast early growth drives total views mechanically. Key question: can day3 growth be predicted from pre-upload features?

hypothesis: META: day3Growth is unpredictable from ALL pre-upload features (max r=0.08). It is the algorithm's pure amplification signal, not a content property. Cannot be added to predictive model.
method: Tested 13 pre-upload features vs day3Growth. All r<0.09.
notes: CRITICAL FINDING. Trajectory signals are powerful descriptors but NOT predictors. first3Share is partially predictable (r=-0.29 from prevKeep), making it the more useful trajectory proxy for prediction.

hypothesis: z(keep) - z(f3share) + z(d3g) + z(esRatio) = 0.681 correlation with views. 46% of view variance from 4 signals: 1 pre-upload quality + 3 trajectory signals.
method: Z-score normalize keep, first3DayShare, day3Growth, endStartRetentionRatio. Combine with sign correction.
notes: THEORETICAL CEILING for trajectory-augmented prediction. But d3g and esRatio are outcome — only keep and partially f3share are pre-upload. Practical 2-signal model: keep + f3share_predicted.

hypothesis: Previous video's keep rate mediates THIS video's trajectory. prevKeep → f3share → views. Mechanism: good prior keep → algorithm gives longer sustained push to next video.
method: Test causal chain: prevKeep → f3share → logViews using mediation analysis
notes: prevKeep predicts f3share (r=-0.29). f3share predicts views (r=-0.41). Combined mediation path. Practical implication: high-keep video → next video gets algorithm runway.

hypothesis: Videos whose retention curves deviate MORE from pure exponential decay get fewer views. Clean, predictable decay = algorithm friendly.
method: Fit log(retention) ~ time for retentionCurve points 5-100%, compute mean absolute residual
notes: r=-0.26, r_partial=-0.19 (370). Smooth curves outperform jagged ones. Complements inflection count and mid-section stability findings.

hypothesis: Mega-viral (20M+) is partially unpredictable. LLM concept scoring has ZERO predictive power (r=-0.05). Model misses 0.435 log-views for 20M+ tier.
notes: Confirmed in Round 5. The gap between 10M and 100M views is algorithmic, not content-driven. Model ceiling at CV=0.664 with linear methods on n=203.

result: STRONGEST SINGLE PREDICTOR. r=+0.60, explains 37% of view variance alone. Log10 of day3-7 avg / day1 views.
notes: Captures algorithmic acceleration. 20M+ videos have 10x higher acceleration than rest.

result: Week2/Week1 view ratio. CV +0.026. Captures sustained trajectory.
notes: Independent of view_accel_log — measures different time horizon.

hypothesis: Rolling 5-video standard deviation of log(views). Lower consistency = more viral potential (suggests experimentation vs formula).
method: Compute std(log10(views)) of previous 5 videos from metadata.
notes: Free feature from metadata. Needs >5 ordered videos.

hypothesis: Smoothness of retention curve transition from hook (0-10%) to body (10-30%). Smoother transition = better hook setup.
method: Compute std of second derivative at the hook/body boundary (segments[0].endTime).
notes: Requires segment timing + retention curve alignment.

hypothesis: Embedding-based title quality score. Project title embedding onto direction from low-view to high-view titles.
method: Get embeddings via OpenAI, compute projection onto top PCA component of views-sorted titles.
notes: Requires embedding API. May capture style/format patterns that keywords miss.

hypothesis: With n=500+ samples, non-linear models (GBM/Ridge) may outperform OLS.
method: Collect 300+ additional videos with full analytics. Re-test GBM/Ridge/poly.
notes: At n=203 all non-linear models underperformed OLS. Data collection is the bottleneck.

hypothesis: Scripts with more reveal/payoff words ('result', 'finally', 'success', 'worked') predict higher views
expected_signal: r > 0.13 vs log(views), Cohen's d=0.92 for 20M+ tier
method: Count reveal vocabulary in transcript: reveal/revealed/answer/result/results/finally/worked/success/winner

hypothesis: Titles with word overlap to top-20 all-time videos predict higher performance
method: Build frequency-weighted word overlap score between title and top-20 video titles

hypothesis: Videos with more dynamic retention curves (higher concavity variation) get more views
method: Compute std of second derivative of retentionCurve

hypothesis: Videos with a bigger single retention drop point get more views (hook filtering effect)
method: Compute max single-point retention drop from retentionCurve

hypothesis: Making/building concept × keep rate interaction predicts views beyond either alone
method: is_making_title × keep_rate

hypothesis: First frame with larger/more dramatic subject relative to frame predicts more views
method: GPT-4o-mini scores first frame for scale_factor (1-10)

hypothesis: GBM captures non-linear feature interactions that boost prediction from CV=0.664 to CV=0.84+
method: GradientBoostingRegressor on all non-trajectory features

hypothesis: Videos with more physical/tangible content words are more universally engaging
method: Count physical action words (build/break/smash/crush/cut/melt etc) in transcript

hypothesis: Scripts with more tension-release sentiment cycles predict higher views
method: Split transcript into 10 segments, compute sentiment per segment, count sign changes

hypothesis: Videos with stronger retention curve recovery moments get more views
method: Max value of second derivative of retentionCurve

hypothesis: Videos ending with positive sentiment in final 10% of transcript predict more views
method: Compute positive - negative word ratio in final 10% of transcript
